_meta.note: 퀴즈(정답형) · BuzzFeed-ish clear tone · glossary 고정 · 곧은따옴표 금지(컬리 ’ “ ”) · js_001은 라이브러리 원문 보존

html_001: Ko Yo-el · 2024 · Self-Directed Learning in the AI Age
html_005: AI is turning the age of credentials into the age of self-study
html_008: and the age of ‘self-study,’ the power to learn on your own, is coming.”
html_010: Master the definition, principles, and tactics of self-study through
html_012:
.
        You start easy and it gradually gets harder,
html_013: and wrong answers keep coming back until you get them right.
html_030: This is where the free preview ends
html_046: and you’ll never forget it. Every starting point is
html_047: a self-set purpose and total mobilization of means
html_050: 🌐 The AI age: not credentials, but self-study
html_051: The power to learn on your own decides the gap
html_053: 🎯 Set your own purpose for learning
html_054: Be clear on why and what you learn
html_059: 🧠 Check with metacognition and repeat diligently
html_060: Separating what you know from what you don’t
html_065: The table of contents at a glance
html_067: 3 cases from the book worth pondering
html_071: has more great talks on learning and metacognition to explore.
html_077: A repeat master quiz that turns a book’s core into dopamine
html_078: This app is secondary learning content that reworks the book’s core concepts for study. For the full reading experience, please turn to the original book.

js_004: Who wrote ‘Self-Study Power — the one power we need in the AI age’?
js_009: The author is Ko Yo-el. He majored in business at the National University of Singapore, yet taught himself AI, ended up lecturing, and even won recognition from global companies and the UK government.
js_011: In what age does this book say ‘self-study’ is exactly the power you need?
js_012: An uncertain age where AI is changing everything
js_013: The early industrial age when factories were multiplying
js_014: The age when the internet was just starting to spread
js_015: A bygone age when everyone aimed only at getting into college
js_016: It is exactly the subtitle: ‘the one power we need in the AI age.’ The more common knowledge becomes, the more the power to learn on your own sets people apart, it says.
js_018: Which is closest to what the author means by ‘self-study power’?
js_019: Setting your own purpose and marshalling every means to learn what you need
js_020: Simply following, without ever asking why, a study timetable that someone else drew up for you
js_021: A natural-born knack for memorizing whatever you read at high speed
js_022: Diligently leaning on cram schools and private tutors to be taught
js_023: Self-study bundles a clear purpose + every means + self-driven learning. The author calls it not talent but the power of diligence.
js_025: In the AI age, what does the author say sets people apart instead of ‘credentials’?
js_026: Self-study — the power to keep learning on your own
js_027: The nameplate of which university you graduated from
js_028: The IQ you happened to be assigned at birth
js_029: How wide a network of connections you have built
js_030: The author says ‘the age of credentials is fading and the age of self-study is coming.’ Not a nameplate but the power to learn on your own becomes your edge.
js_032: What ability did the author single out as common to Musk, Gates, and Zuckerberg?
js_033: Self-study that reached beyond the fence of their major
js_034: An elite university diploma that everyone envies
js_035: A huge fortune inherited from wealthy, enviable parents
js_036: A lucky, perfectly-timed moment to start a company
js_037: These world-changing founders taught themselves fields they never studied in school and opened new industries. The author sees self-study as their common thread.
js_039: What common myth about ‘self-study’ does the author correct?
js_040: That you must do it all alone, struggling with no one’s help
js_041: That you should set a clear purpose before you begin to learn
js_042: That being consistent matters more than anything else does
js_043: That you have to produce a tangible result in the end
js_044: Self-study is not ‘studying in isolation.’ Real self-study, the author stresses, mobilizes every means — books, lectures, people, even AI.
js_046: What did the author stress as the ‘very first thing’ to do in self-study?
js_047: Setting a clear purpose — why and what you will learn
js_048: Stocking up on pricey textbooks and courses first
js_049: Cranking your daily study load far above everyone else
js_050: Just copying whatever study everyone else is doing
js_051: If the purpose is blurry, the method wobbles too. Deciding ‘for what, and what’ to learn first is where self-study begins.
js_053: In the definition of self-study, what is the right attitude toward ‘means and methods’?
js_054: Mobilize every means you can — books, lectures, people, AI
js_055: Stubbornly stick to a single paper book and nothing else
js_056: Follow only what school teaches in scheduled class hours
js_057: Never touch a search engine or any other tool at all
js_058: You use anything that helps you reach your purpose. Holding nothing back — total mobilization — is the core attitude of self-study.
js_060: In the end, the author calls self-study ‘the power of what’?
js_061: The power of diligence, seeing it through toward a purpose
js_062: The power of genius that memorizes it all in one look
js_063: The power of last-minute cramming right before a test
js_064: The power of clever little shortcuts and quick tricks
js_065: The author nails it down: self-study is the power of diligence. Not sparkling talent but consistency builds real skill.
js_067: Which approach did the author see as more effective for self-study?
js_068: Building up a little every day, consistently
js_069: Cramming it all in right before the test
js_070: Diving in deep only when you feel like it
js_071: Pushing many times the usual load all at once
js_072: ‘A little every day, consistently’ beats ‘once, short and deep.’ Only repetition makes knowledge truly yours.
js_074: Kyobo Book Centre — ‘Self-Study Power’ book info
js_086: Ko Yo-el was a business student at the National University of Singapore, with no formal training in computer science or AI. By pure self-study he became an AI expert and took the lectern.
js_087: The more AI equalizes access to knowledge, the wider the gap in ‘the power to learn and use it yourself’ grows — that is this book’s central concern.
js_088: Self-study = a clear purpose + every means + self-driven learning. Bundling these three into ‘the power of diligence’ is the heart of this book.
js_089: ‘Credentials (a nameplate)’ barely change once fixed, but ‘self-study’ can grow for a lifetime. That makes it a stronger weapon in an age of change.
js_090: Musk taught himself much of rocket science; Gates and Zuckerberg, much of programming. Their common thread is ‘the power to dig in on your own,’ not their major.
js_091: The myth that ‘self-study means isolation’ wears people out. Asking people and taking lectures are all means of self-study too. It is not about suffering alone.
js_092: If the purpose is vague, you can’t decide what or how much to learn. Only when ‘why you learn’ is clear do the method, amount, and order follow.
js_093: The moment you pick and choose means, learning narrows. Total mobilization means pulling in anything that helps — books, video, people, AI, hands-on practice.
js_094: Why the author calls self-study ‘the power of diligence’ — because it takes not a special mind but ‘the attitude of steadily seeing it through,’ open to anyone.
js_095: ‘A little every day’ wins because of forgetting. Meeting it briefly and often catches it again before it slips, moving it into long-term memory.
js_109: The power to set your own purpose, mobilize every means, and learn what you need on your own initiative. The author calls it ‘the power of diligence.’
js_111: The more AI equalizes access to knowledge, the more the power to learn and use it yourself sets people apart.
js_113: Only when why and what you learn is clear do method, amount, and order follow. A blurry purpose makes self-study wobble.
js_114: A self-check that separates what you know from what you don’t. Clearing away the illusion of knowing lets you fill the gaps precisely.
js_116: If you can’t explain it in plain words, you don’t really know it. Where your explanation stalls is exactly what you don’t understand.
js_118: AI is the flagship tool of total mobilization. Ask well and it’s a 24/7 private tutor; copy only the answers and nothing sticks.
js_120: Input alone never becomes skill. Only by making something — writing, code, a work — does knowledge harden into a usable form.
js_122: The belief that ‘ability grows.’ The attitude that consistency, not talent, builds skill is what sustains self-study.
js_124: How a business student self-taught his way to the AI lectern
js_125: Author Ko Yo-el was a business major who had never formally studied computer science or AI.
js_126: Offered a chance to teach AI, he dug in by self-study alone, took the lectern, and won recognition as an expert.
js_127: The conviction he drew from it — the real weapon of the AI age is not credentials but ‘self-study.’
js_128: The question running through the book: when no one teaches you, can you learn on your own?
js_130: Why ‘self-study’ now — the age of credentials is fading
js_131: The more AI makes knowledge common, the more the power to learn and use it yourself creates the gap.
js_132: A ‘nameplate’ you once earned goes stale fast. In an age of change you must keep updating yourself.
js_133: What Musk, Gates, and Zuckerberg share — they opened industries by self-study, beyond their majors.
js_134: This isn’t about rejecting school — it’s about adding self-study on top of it.
js_136: The myths of self-study, and why studying used to fail
js_137: The myth that ‘self-study means going it alone’ wears you out. People and lectures are means of self-study too.
js_138: Start without a purpose and you lose direction. Study missing the ‘why’ never lasts.
js_139: Weak metacognition traps you in the ‘illusion of knowing,’ so you walk right past what you don’t grasp.
js_140: Repeat only reading and listening (input) and never make anything (output), and skill never sticks.
js_142: How to build self-study — mindset, tactics, and output
js_143: The growth mindset that ‘ability grows’ comes first. It turns failure into material for learning.
js_144: It mobilizes concrete tactics — setting a purpose, good questions, the Feynman technique, AI tutors, verifying information.
js_145: Repeat daily, however small, to build a habit, and push through to the end while managing stress.
js_146: Last comes output — make something from what you learned to prove your skill and fill the gaps.
js_148: A business major, the author had never formally studied AI, yet when offered a chance to teach he dug in by pure self-study and took the lectern. He went on to have his expertise recognized by global companies and the UK government.
js_149: The author himself proves the book’s claim: ‘even outside your major, self-study can carry you to the top.’
js_150: Founders who opened industries outside their majors
js_151: Elon Musk taught himself much of rocket science; Bill Gates and Mark Zuckerberg, much of programming — and each built a new industry. It was digging-in self-study, more than any degree, that made them.
js_152: A prime example showing that the shared key to success may be ‘self-study,’ not ‘credentials.’
js_153: The AI age, where anyone can have a private tutor
js_154: In the past you needed a good teacher or academy to learn; now you can ask AI, request explanations, and learn on your own. The tool is the same, but the result splits on ‘how you ask and use it.’
js_155: It makes you realize that as opportunity levels out, ‘the power to learn and use it yourself’ becomes the more decisive difference.
js_156: Have I only followed a curriculum others set, or have I ever set my own purpose?
js_157: Pick one thing you want to learn now and write ‘why’ in a single sentence. The purpose will pull the method along.
js_158: Am I only piling up input (reading, listening), or am I proving it with output (something I made)?
js_159: Turn one thing you learned this week into a short note, memo, or small result. Only by making it does your skill show.
js_160: Do I use AI as a lever for learning, or as a copier that just spits out answers?
js_161: Ask AI for the ‘explanation’ and the ‘why,’ not just the ‘answer.’ The power to question for yourself grows your self-study.
js_163: You’ve fully mastered the power to learn on your own in the AI age
js_165: You’ve got almost all of self-study’s core principles down
js_167: You’ve got the framework — one more round!
js_169: The more you repeat, the higher your score climbs

_app.title: Self-Study Power — Master Quiz
_app.desc: Master self-study — the one power you need in the AI age — through a quiz.
